Week 01. Introduction, Formal Definition of the Language of Propoitional Logic for CS245

11 January 2021 - 2 mins read time
Tags: CS 245

Source: University of Waterloo CS 245 by Professor Shai Ben-David

What is Logic?

It focuses on how to reliably get from assumptions to conclusions.

Example: \(F=ma\)

  • Physician: True or False
  • Mathematician: If \(F=ma\) then \(a=\frac{F}{m}\)
  • Logician: If whenever \(X=yz\) then \(y =\frac{X}{z}\). This statement works then if \(F=ma\) then \(a=\frac{F}{m}\)

Example of Logical Argument

  • Socrates(first logician): If all men are mortal and I am a man, then I am mortal.

  • Russell’s Paradox: The baraber in the village shaves every man in the village that does not shave themselves. Does the barber shave himself?

  • Berry’s Paradox:

    Number that can be precisely described by an English sentence:

    • ten
    • ten to the power ten
    • the number of hairs on the heads of all students in the class
    • 721638354812638

    B = all numbers that can be described by an English Sentence of at most 200 characters. Is B a finite set?

    Yes, since there are at most 26 ^200 such combinations of characters. “The first number that cannot be described by an English Sentence of at most 200 characters”

  • More example:

1. At least one of the statements in this box is false.
2. Mark is not going to be millionaire by the age of 25

Goal

  • Develop Reliable tools for going from assumptions to implied conclusions.
  • Do not rely on any Natural Language for that purpose.
  • Constructing(and analyzing) formal languages.
    • Propositional Logic: \(p\lor q \implies s\)
    • First Order Logic: \(\forall x \exists y(y > x)\)
    • etc.

Syntax vs Sementics

Syntax: Form of the language Sementics: Meaning of the language

Syntax of Propositional Logic

Defined the set of all valid Words in this language.

What tools do we have for formally or precisely defining a set?

  1. Make a list of all members of the set. (0,2,4,…)
  2. By a common characteristic. (number that are divisible by 2)

Inductive Definitions of Sets

It is consists of followings:

  1. Universe set: \(X\)
  2. Core set: \(A\subseteq X\)
  3. Set of Operations(\(P\)): Functions from \(X\times X \times ...\times X \to X\). Requires that \(P\) is finite.

\(B\subseteq X\) is closed under the operations in \(P\), if for every \(b_1..bn \in B\) and \(f \in P\), \(f(b_1, ...,b_k) \in X\)

\(I(A,P)\)

The set defined inductively from \(X,A,P\) is denoted \(I(A,P)\) is the smallest subset of \(X\) that contains \(A\) and is closed under \(P\).

Exampel

\[X = Real\space Number , A= {5}, P = \{f1: x->x+1, f2: x->x-1\}\] \[I(A,P) = All \space Integers\]

Does such smallest set always exist? Yes.

\[I(A,P)= \cap\{B \subseteq X: A \subseteq B, B closed under P\}\]

Recall: Given a Set \(W\), \(\cap W = {x: \forall B \in W, x \in B}\)

Why does the intersection satisfies the requirements of \(I(A,P)\)?

  1. \(A \subseteq \cap\{B \subseteq X: A \subseteq B, B closed under P\}\). Since \(x \in A\) implies \(x \in B\) for all \(B\) in \(W\)

To be Continued
